MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Jun-29-2016 Denied
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT AEROJET-GENERAL CORPORATION'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

Appearances: Kimberly Chu of Brayton Purcell (415.898.1555) for plaintiff and responding party; Wynne Shatkus of Dal Soglio & Martens LLP (310.662.4748) for defendant and moving party.

Argued and the Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Aerojet-General Corporation's motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendant failed to sustain its initial burden of demonstrating that plaintiff does not possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence that he was exposed to asbestos-containing products or materials attributable to defendant. Further, defendant's moving papers never argued that plaintiff's identification of them as Aerojet employees is inadmissible. However, having considered the objection, plaintiff's identification of Aerojet employees is admissible. Plaintiff testified that Aerojet employees exposed him to pipe insulation that contained asbestos, and defendant failed to demonstrate that plaintiff does not have knowledge that the insulation at issue contained asbestos. Defendant is precluded from successfully invoking the factually devoid prong of Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826 for this issue. Defendant failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that it is not liable for injuries sustained by plaintiff while on defendant's premises. Defendant failed to demonstrate that the premises liability authority it relies on bars plaintiff's claims against it. Plaintiff's deposition testimony and special interrogatory responses preclude defendant from sustaining its initial burden because it demonstrates that defendant's alleged liability is based on the affirmative conduct of defendant's employees, not on the theories discussed in defendant's authority. That is, even if defendant is correct that plaintiff cannot demonstrate that defendant failed to warn of a concealed, pre-existing hazardous condition on the premises or negligently exercised control over plaintiff's work, defendant still would not demonstrate that plaintiff cannot successfully assert his Negligence II cause of action against it. (See Biles v. Exxon Mobil Corp. (2004) 124 Cal.App.4th 1315.) Finally, defendant failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that any liability for asbestos exposure on the Aerojet premises rests with plaintiff's employer pursuant to SeaBright Ins. Co. v. US Airways, Inc. (2011) 52 Cal.4th 590. Defendant's objections to excerpts of plaintiff's deposition testimony are overruled. Order signed. Judge: Garrett L. Wong, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Reporter: Angela Sinclair, CSR 13902 (Aiken Welch, 877.451.1580) (503/GLW)
